title: The Slowdown of R-CNN
concept: R-CNN is too slow for real-time applications because it runs 2,000 separate CNN passes per image and duplicates feature computation.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does R-CNN train multiple SVMs?

options.text: Because it trains a separate binary classifier for each object class
To calculate bounding box regression offsets
To resize the proposed region crops

exercises.explain: R-CNN trains a separate one-vs-all binary SVM for each target category.

cards.title: Waiting for Results
cards.body: If you are reading a book, and you look up every single word in a heavy paper dictionary, it will take you hours to finish a page. You are wasting massive time because you lookup overlapping letters and prefixes repeatedly. This is how R-CNN wastes time.

cards.title: R-CNN Latency
cards.body: Original R-CNN Latency is extremely high. Processing a single image takes about 47 seconds on a GPU. This is far too slow for real-time applications like self-driving cars, which require processing 30 frames per second.

cards.title: Reading one word at a time
cards.body: Instead of reading a sentence as a single unit, you cut it into 2,000 separate words and analyze each word in a different room. You spend all your energy walking between rooms rather than understanding the text!

cards.title: Disk Space Nightmare
cards.body: Because R-CNN trains modules separately, researchers had to run the CNN on all training images, extract feature vectors for 2,000 crops per image, and save them to disk! This required hundreds of gigabytes of storage for small datasets.

cards.title: Not just Selective Search's fault
cards.body: You might think the CPU proposal step is the only reason R-CNN is slow. While Selective Search takes 2 seconds, the 2,000 separate CNN forward passes take 45 seconds! The primary bottleneck is the repeated neural network computation.

cards.title: Redundant Computations
cards.body: Visualizing how R-CNN duplicates features for overlapping crops:

left.text:
Many proposals overlap by 90%:
Crops share the same pixels.

right.text:
R-CNN runs CNN on both:
Calculates same features twice.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary cause of R-CNN's massive latency?
exercises.explain: The main bottleneck is executing 2,000 separate forward passes through the CNN for each image's proposed crops.

options.text: It runs the CNN feature extractor 2,000 separate times for 2,000 crops per image
It can only process grayscale images
Linear SVMs require complex gradient descent
It does not support data augmentation

exercises.prompt: True or False: Original R-CNN requires storing hundreds of gigabytes of intermediate feature vectors on disk during training.
exercises.explain: True. Because the modules are separate, R-CNN extracts and saves feature vectors for every region proposal to disk before training the SVMs.
R-CNN takes nearly 47 seconds per image, making it highly impractical for production.
exercises.sentence: R-CNN takes about ___ seconds to process a single image on a GPU.

exercises.prompt: Which aspect of R-CNN represents a redundant calculation?
exercises.explain: Processing overlapping crops independently forces the CNN to compute the same visual features multiple times.

options.text: Running the CNN independently on multiple crops that overlap by 95%
Using a single shared backbone for features
Running NMS post-processing to clean up boxes

exercises.prompt: You want to design a new network to replace R-CNN. To solve the speed bottleneck, what core change should you make to feature extraction?

options.text: Run the CNN once on the entire image to generate a shared feature map, and project the proposals onto it.
Use a smaller CNN backbone that takes 10x10 pixel inputs.
Run the Selective Search algorithm on a GPU instead of CPU.
Bypass feature extraction entirely and use raw pixels.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Processing the entire image once and sharing the feature map eliminates the redundant 2,000 CNN runs. Next: let's study the network that implemented this exact fix: Fast R-CNN!
